title: Usage
text:
This section explains how to use the Air Analyzer project.

cd air_analyzer



Open the firmware project inside the development environment and build it.



Describe here how to flash the microcontroller.

title: Hardware
text:
This section describes the hardware architecture of Air Analyzer, a low-power wearable system designed to acquire breathing-related pressure variations inside a sports mask, store the acquired data locally, and allow post-session data retrieval through a wired interface.
The main design goal is to avoid continuous wireless transmission during acquisition. Pressure samples are stored in a local non-volatile memory and downloaded only after the recording session. This reduces the average power consumption and makes the system suitable for operation from a compact coin cell battery.

title: Hardware
text:
USB-C connector
The USB-C connector is used for post-session communication with a PC. During a recording session, the system does not stream data continuously. Instead, pressure samples are stored in the external EEPROM and retrieved later.



USB-C interface schematic


USB-C connector used for post-session data retrieval.

title: Hardware
text:
STDC14 and UART connector
The board also includes a debug and development interface based on the STDC14 connector and a dedicated UART connector. These interfaces simplify firmware development, programming, and serial debugging.



UART connector schematic


UART connector used for debugging and development communication.
The STDC14 connector provides access to the SWD programming interface, reset line, supply reference, and serial communication lines. This allows the board to be programmed and tested without relying on the battery during development.

title: Air Analyzer
text:
Air Analyzer


Wearable Smart Mask for Breathing Activity Monitoring


Air Analyzer is an embedded system designed to monitor breathing-related activity using a microcontroller-based architecture.
The project focuses on sensor acquisition, firmware development, interrupt-based logic, and documentation of the embedded workflow.







Project Sections



Project Overview
General description of the Air Analyzer project, including motivation, objectives, and repository structure.
Open ‚Üí


Hardware
Description of the hardware architecture, sensor connections, microcontroller board, and physical setup.
Open ‚Üí

Explanation of the embedded firmware, main source files, interrupt-based approach, and communication logic.

Usage
Instructions for cloning, building, flashing, and using the project.

title: Overview
text:
Air Analyzer is a low-power wearable system designed for respiratory activity monitoring during physical exercise. The device is integrated into a sports mask and uses an embedded microcontroller-based architecture to acquire pressure-related breathing signals, store data locally, and allow post-session data retrieval through USB.
The main objective of the project is to provide a compact, non-invasive, and energy-efficient solution for monitoring breathing patterns in athletes and during physical activity.



Respiratory activity is an important physiological indicator during exercise. Breathing frequency and breathing patterns can provide useful information about physical effort, fatigue level, and cardiopulmonary response to training.
Many existing respiratory monitoring systems are either too bulky for sport applications, uncomfortable during movement, expensive, or dependent on wireless communication. These limitations reduce their usability in real exercise conditions.
Air Analyzer addresses these issues by combining:

direct pressure sensing inside a sports mask;
local data storage;
low-power firmware management;
USB-based post-session data retrieval;
a compact custom PCB suitable for wearable integration.





The key idea of Air Analyzer is to place a barometric pressure sensor inside a sports mask in order to detect the small pressure variations caused by inhalation and exhalation.
Instead of continuously transmitting data through Bluetooth or Wi-Fi, the system stores the acquired samples locally inside an EEPROM memory. After the recording session, the user can connect the device to a PC through USB-C and retrieve the stored data for offline analysis.
This design choice reduces power consumption and makes the system suitable for battery-powered operation.




The system is based on the following workflow:
Breathing activity
        ‚Üì
Pressure variation inside the mask
        ‚Üì
LPS22HBTR barometric pressure sensor
        ‚Üì
STM32L052 microcontroller
        ‚Üì
SPI EEPROM local memory
        ‚Üì
USB-C data retrieval
        ‚Üì
Offline analysis




The main goals of the project are:

acquire respiratory-related pressure signals during physical activity;
reduce power consumption through an interrupt-driven firmware strategy;
avoid continuous wireless transmission;
store data locally during the recording session;
retrieve data after the session through USB-C;
design a compact PCB suitable for integration into a sports mask;
provide a reproducible hardware and firmware platform.





Air Analyzer is based on three main design choices.


The pressure sensor is placed directly inside the mask. This allows the system to detect breathing-related pressure variations without requiring chest belts, skin-contact electrodes, or external laboratory equipment.



The system stores data locally on an SPI EEPROM. This avoids the need for continuous Bluetooth or Wi-Fi transmission, reducing energy consumption and simplifying the device architecture.



The microcontroller spends most of the time in a low-power mode and wakes up only when needed. The sensor FIFO generates an interrupt when enough samples are available, allowing the firmware to read data in bursts instead of continuously polling the sensor.





The project includes three main parts:

Hardware: custom PCB with microcontroller, pressure sensor, EEPROM memory, power supply, and USB-C interface.
Firmware: interrupt-based embedded software for sensor acquisition, data storage, and communication.
Mechanical integration: sports mask structure designed to host the PCB and position the sensor correctly.





The repository is organized as follows:
air_analyzer/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ embedded/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ micro_usb/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ docs/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ index.qmd
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ overview.qmd
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ hardware.qmd
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ firmware.qmd
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ usage.qmd
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md
The firmware implementation is mainly located inside the micro_usb/ directory, while the Quarto documentation is generated inside the docs/ folder.

title: Firmware
text:
This section describes the embedded firmware of the Air Analyzer system.
The firmware is designed to acquire the pressure signal generated by breathing inside the mask, store the raw samples locally, and reduce CPU activity during idle periods. The current implementation follows a simple low-power, interrupt-driven architecture based on STM32CubeMX, STM32 HAL, SPI, and DMA.


Firmware Overview
The firmware is built around three main ideas:

configure the pressure sensor once at startup;
keep the main program inactive during idle time;
handle acquisition and storage only when an event occurs.

This approach avoids continuous CPU polling. The microcontroller remains mostly inactive and wakes only when data must be moved from the pressure sensor to the external memory.
The current firmware acquires only the pressure signal. Temperature data are not acquired, because the respiratory waveform is extracted from pressure variations inside the mask.



Development Environment
The firmware was developed starting from an STM32CubeMX project.
CubeMX was used to generate the basic configuration for:

system clock;
GPIO pins;
SPI communication;
DMA support;
USART interface for debugging;
USB interface for post-session communication.

The application-specific code is added inside the user-code sections generated by CubeMX, so that the project can be regenerated without overwriting the custom firmware logic.



Project Structure
The firmware is located mainly in the following project folder:
micro_usb/
The main application logic is located in:
micro_usb/Core/Src/main.c
The configuration and peripheral initialization files are generated by CubeMX and organized in the standard STM32 project structure.



Firmware Architecture
The firmware can be divided into three main parts:
Startup and initialization
          ‚Üì
Low-power main loop
          ‚Üì
Interrupt-driven acquisition and storage
The initialization code is executed only once when the device starts. After that, the firmware enters a cyclic acquisition workflow in which SPI transfers are performed using DMA and completed through interrupts.



Initialization Phase
At startup, the microcontroller initializes the peripherals configured in STM32CubeMX. This includes the clock tree, GPIO pins, SPI interface, DMA controller, USB interface, and USART interface.
After the peripheral setup, the pressure sensor is configured for continuous acquisition at 10 Hz. This sampling frequency was chosen because it provides a large margin with respect to the expected respiratory frequency during physical activity, while keeping memory usage limited.
Only the pressure output of the LPS22HBTR is acquired. The pressure value is provided by the sensor as a 24-bit raw sample, so each acquired pressure sample occupies exactly 3 bytes.
Once the sensor has been configured, the first SPI acquisition is started. From this point on, the firmware operates in an event-driven way rather than continuously polling the sensor.



Low-Power Main Loop
The main loop is intentionally kept almost empty.
Instead of continuously checking whether new sensor data are available, the microcontroller waits for interrupts. During this waiting phase, the CPU can remain inactive, reducing unnecessary power consumption.
This behavior is important for a wearable battery-powered system. The firmware does not require the processor to be active during the entire recording session. The CPU only needs to wake up briefly when a transfer is completed and a new pressure sample must be handled.
The low-power strategy is therefore based on event-driven execution:
CPU waits
   ‚Üì
DMA transfer completes
   ‚Üì
Interrupt occurs
   ‚Üì
Sample is stored
   ‚Üì
CPU returns to waiting state



Interrupt-Driven Acquisition
The acquisition process is managed using SPI with DMA support.
DMA allows the SPI peripheral to transfer data without requiring the CPU to manually handle every byte. When the transfer is complete, an interrupt is generated and the firmware processes the acquired sample.
At each acquisition event, the firmware:

receives the raw pressure sample from the LPS22HBTR;
extracts the useful pressure bytes;
prepares the data for storage;
writes the sample to the external EEPROM;
starts the next acquisition cycle.

This creates a cyclic acquisition pipeline in which the CPU is active only for short processing intervals.



Pressure Data Format
The LPS22HBTR pressure output is stored as a 24-bit raw value.
The firmware stores this raw value directly in EEPROM without converting it to floating-point pressure values during acquisition. This choice reduces computational overhead and avoids unnecessary processing on the microcontroller.
Each stored sample has the following size:
1 pressure sample = 3 bytes
The conversion to physical pressure values is performed later during offline analysis on a PC.
The pressure conversion is:
pressure_hPa = raw_pressure / 4096
Storing raw samples is convenient because it preserves the full sensor resolution while minimizing memory usage.



External Memory Storage
The system uses an M95256 SPI EEPROM for local data storage.
The EEPROM capacity is:
256 Kbit = 32 KB = 32768 bytes
Since each pressure sample occupies 3 bytes and the sampling frequency is 10 Hz, the approximate recording time is:
32768 bytes / (3 bytes/sample √ó 10 samples/s) ‚âà 1092 s ‚âà 18 min
This storage strategy avoids continuous wireless transmission during the recording session. Data are saved locally and can be retrieved after the session through the communication interface.



Data Retrieval
During acquisition, the device does not continuously stream data to an external receiver.
The recorded pressure samples are stored locally in EEPROM. After the recording session, the user can connect the device to a PC and download the stored data for offline processing.
This design choice reduces power consumption because no Bluetooth or Wi-Fi transmission is required during exercise.



Offline Processing
The firmware does not compute the breathing frequency in real time.
Instead, it stores the pressure waveform in raw format. The respiratory signal can then be analyzed offline to extract:

inhalation and exhalation cycles;
breathing frequency;
pressure waveform shape;
differences between subjects or exercise conditions.

This separation between acquisition and analysis simplifies the embedded firmware and reduces the processing load on the microcontroller.



Low-Power Design Rationale
The firmware contributes to low-power operation through three main mechanisms.







Strategy
Purpose




Local storage
Avoids continuous wireless transmission


DMA-based SPI communication
Reduces CPU involvement during data transfer


Interrupt-driven execution
Keeps the CPU inactive during idle periods



Together, these choices allow the firmware to acquire respiratory pressure data while limiting active processor time.



Current Firmware Summary



Parameter
Value




Microcontroller
STM32L052K8Tx


Firmware generation tool
STM32CubeMX


Main firmware file
micro_usb/Core/Src/main.c


Sensor
LPS22HBTR


Acquired quantity
Pressure only


Sensor interface
SPI


Transfer method
DMA-based SPI


Sampling frequency
10 Hz


Stored sample format
Raw 24-bit pressure


Bytes per sample
3 bytes


External memory
M95256 SPI EEPROM


Recording duration
Approximately 18 minutes


Main low-power mechanism
Interrupt-driven waiting loop
